sentences.translation: Once upon a time there was a prince.

words.grammar: være (to be) · var (past) · har været (perfect) · "Der var engang" = once upon a time, the fairy-tale opening formula
en prins · prinsen (the prince) · prinser (plural) · prinserne (the princes)

sentences.text: Han ville gerne have en rigtig prinsesse.
sentences.translation: He wanted to have a real princess.

words.grammar: ville (modal: want to/would) · present: vil · ville gerne = would like to · modal verb, no "at" before the following infinitive
rigtig (common) · rigtigt (neuter) · rigtige (pl./def.) · en rigtig prinsesse = a true (genuine) princess, not just any princess

sentences.text: Han rejste over hele verden, men han fandt ingen.
sentences.translation: He traveled all over the world, but he found none.

words.grammar: rejse (to travel) · rejste (past) · har rejst (perfect) · weak verb, regular -te ending
ingen (for en-words/persons) · intet (for et-words) · negative pronoun, replaces a noun + negation together

sentences.text: En aften kom der en prinsesse til slottet.
sentences.translation: One evening a princess came to the castle.

words.grammar: komme (to come) · kom (past) · er kommet (perfect) · strong verb, vowel change but no -te ending
et slot · slottet (the castle) · slotte (plural) · slottene (the castles)

sentences.text: Det regnede, og hun var meget våd.
sentences.translation: It was raining, and she was very wet.

words.grammar: regne (to rain) · regnede (past) · har regnet (perfect) · weak verb
våd (common) · vådt (neuter) · våde (pl./def.)

sentences.translation: The queen said: "We must test her."

words.grammar: skulle (modal) · skal (present) · used for plans, decisions, and instructions · skal teste = is going to test
en dronning · dronningen (the queen) · dronninger (plural)

sentences.text: Dronningen lagde en ært under tyve madrasser.
sentences.translation: The queen put a pea under twenty mattresses.

words.grammar: tyve (20) · cardinal number · combines directly with plural noun, e.g. tyve madrasser
en ært · ærten (the pea) · ærter (plural)

sentences.text: Prinsessen sov på sengen om natten.
sentences.translation: The princess slept in the bed during the night.

words.grammar: sove (to sleep) · sov (past) · har sovet (perfect) · strong verb
en nat · natten (the night) · om natten = during the night

sentences.text: Om morgenen spurgte dronningen: "Hvordan sov du?"
sentences.translation: In the morning the queen asked: "How did you sleep?"

words.grammar: spørge (to ask) · spurgte (past) · har spurgt (perfect) · strong/irregular verb

sentences.text: Prinsessen sagde: "Jeg sov dårligt. Der var noget hårdt i sengen."
sentences.translation: The princess said: "I slept badly. There was something hard in the bed."

words.grammar: dårlig (bad, adj.) · dårligt (adverb / neuter form) · opposite of godt/godt
hård (common) · hårdt (neuter) · hårde (pl./def.) · agrees with "noget" (neuter)

sentences.text: Nu vidste de, at hun var en rigtig prinsesse.
sentences.translation: Now they knew that she was a real princess.

words.grammar: vide (to know - facts) · vidste (past) · har vidst (perfect) · irregular, do not confuse with "kende" (know a person/place)

sentences.text: Prinsen og prinsessen blev gift og levede lykkeligt.
sentences.translation: The prince and the princess got married and lived happily.

words.grammar: blive (to become) · blev (past) · er blevet (perfect) · blive gift = to get married
lykkelig (happy, adj.) · lykkeligt (adverb/neuter)

metadata.adaptedFrom: Adapted from H.C. Andersen (1835), simplified for A1 learners

grammarPoints.explanation: This fixed phrase always opens a Danish eventyr, just like "once upon a time" in English. It uses the past tense of være (var) and the dummy word der.

grammarPoints.rule: Modal verb "skal" for plans and decisions
grammarPoints.explanation: Skal expresses an intention or instruction, not just future time. It signals "we are going to, as a decision" — stronger and more deliberate than just predicting what will happen.

grammarPoints.rule: Question word order: verb before subject
grammarPoints.explanation: After a question word (hvordan, hvad, hvor...), the verb comes immediately, then the subject. There is no extra "do/did" word like in English.
Ingen replaces an entire noun phrase plus its negation — "ingen prinsesse" becomes just "ingen". Use intet instead for et-words.

grammarPoints.rule: Numbers combine directly with plural nouns
grammarPoints.exampleSentence: Dronningen lagde en ært under tyve madrasser.
grammarPoints.explanation: Danish needs no linking word between a number and the noun it counts: tyve madrasser, not "tyve af madrasser".
Blive describes a change of state, contrasted with være, which describes an existing state. Blive gift = to get married; være gift = to be married.
